input.text:
# Feature Requirements: Device Performance Monitoring

**Feature ID:** DPM-001  
**Priority:** High  
**Status:** Draft  
**Created:** 2026-05-16  
**Owner:** Engineering Team

---

## 1. Overview

### 1.1 Feature Summary
Real-time and historical monitoring of Android device performance metrics including CPU usage, RAM usage, battery level, and temperature. The system will provide visualization, alerting, reporting, and multi-device comparison capabilities.

### 1.2 Business Goals
- Enable proactive device health monitoring to prevent test failures
- Provide performance insights for device farm optimization
- Support capacity planning through historical data analysis
- Reduce device downtime through early warning alerts
- Enable performance comparison across device models and configurations

### 1.3 Success Metrics
- Performance data latency < 2 seconds from device to UI
- Support monitoring of 100+ concurrent devices
- Alert delivery within 5 seconds of threshold breach
- Historical data retention for 90 days minimum
- 99.9% uptime for monitoring service

---

## 2. Functional Requirements

### 2.1 Real-Time Performance Monitoring

#### 2.1.1 Metrics Collection
**REQ-DPM-001:** The system SHALL collect the following metrics from each connected device:
- CPU usage (percentage, per-core breakdown)
- RAM usage (used/total MB, percentage)
- Battery level (percentage)
- Battery temperature (Celsius)
- Device temperature (Celsius, if available)

**REQ-DPM-002:** Metrics SHALL be collected at configurable intervals with a default of 1 second and maximum of 10 seconds.

**REQ-DPM-003:** The system SHALL use ADB commands for local devices:
- `adb shell dumpsys cpuinfo` for CPU metrics
- `adb shell dumpsys meminfo` for RAM metrics
- `adb shell dumpsys battery` for battery metrics
- `adb shell cat /sys/class/thermal/thermal_zone*/temp` for temperature

**REQ-DPM-004:** The system SHALL support both local (ADB) and cloud-connected devices with the same metric collection interface.

**REQ-DPM-005:** If metric collection fails for a device, the system SHALL retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff before marking the metric as unavailable.

#### 2.1.2 Real-Time Display
**REQ-DPM-006:** The UI SHALL display current performance metrics with visual indicators:
- Numeric values with units
- Progress bars or gauges for percentage-based metrics
- Color coding: green (normal), yellow (warning), red (critical)

**REQ-DPM-007:** The UI SHALL update performance displays within 2 seconds of metric collection.

**REQ-DPM-008:** The system SHALL use WebSocket connections for real-time metric streaming to the UI.

**REQ-DPM-009:** The UI SHALL display the last update timestamp for each metric.

**REQ-DPM-010:** If a device becomes disconnected, the UI SHALL display a "disconnected" state and stop updating metrics.

### 2.2 Historical Data Tracking

#### 2.2.1 Data Storage
**REQ-DPM-011:** The system SHALL store all collected metrics in PostgreSQL with the following schema:
- device_id (foreign key)
- metric_type (cpu, ram, battery_level, temperature)
- metric_value (numeric)
- timestamp (UTC)
- metadata (JSON for additional context)

**REQ-DPM-012:** The system SHALL implement time-series partitioning for performance metrics by day.

**REQ-DPM-013:** The system SHALL retain raw metrics for 7 days, then aggregate to 1-minute averages for 30 days, then 1-hour averages for 90 days.

**REQ-DPM-014:** The system SHALL implement automatic data cleanup for metrics older than 90 days.

**REQ-DPM-015:** The system SHALL use Redis for caching recent metrics (last 5 minutes) to reduce database load.

#### 2.2.2 Data Visualization
**REQ-DPM-016:** The UI SHALL provide time-series charts for each metric type with the following capabilities:
- Selectable time ranges: 1 hour, 6 hours, 24 hours, 7 days, 30 days
- Zoom and pan functionality
- Multi-metric overlay on single chart
- Tooltip showing exact values on hover

**REQ-DPM-017:** Charts SHALL use a charting library (e.g., Chart.js, Recharts, or Apache ECharts) optimized for time-series data.

**REQ-DPM-018:** The system SHALL support exporting chart data as CSV or JSON.

**REQ-DPM-019:** The UI SHALL display statistical summaries for selected time ranges:
- Average, minimum, maximum values
- Standard deviation
- Percentiles (50th, 95th, 99th)

### 2.3 Alert System

#### 2.3.1 Threshold Configuration
**REQ-DPM-020:** Users SHALL be able to configure alert thresholds for each metric type:
- CPU usage > X%
- RAM usage > X%
- Battery level < X%
- Temperature > X°C

**REQ-DPM-021:** Thresholds SHALL support two levels: warning and critical.

**REQ-DPM-022:** Thresholds SHALL be configurable globally (all devices) or per-device.

**REQ-DPM-023:** The system SHALL provide default threshold values:
- CPU: warning 80%, critical 95%
- RAM: warning 85%, critical 95%
- Battery: warning 20%, critical 10%
- Temperature: warning 40°C, critical 50°C

**REQ-DPM-024:** Threshold configurations SHALL be stored in PostgreSQL and cached in Redis.

#### 2.3.2 Alert Generation
**REQ-DPM-025:** The system SHALL evaluate thresholds in real-time as metrics are collected.

**REQ-DPM-026:** Alerts SHALL be generated when a metric exceeds a threshold for 3 consecutive readings to avoid false positives.

**REQ-DPM-027:** The system SHALL implement alert deduplication with a 5-minute cooldown period per device per metric.

**REQ-DPM-028:** Each alert SHALL contain:
- Device ID and name
- Metric type and current value
- Threshold value and level (warning/critical)
- Timestamp
- Alert ID (unique)

#### 2.3.3 Alert Delivery
**REQ-DPM-029:** Alerts SHALL be delivered through the following channels:
- In-app notifications (real-time via WebSocket)
- Desktop notifications (Tauri notification API)
- Alert history log (stored in database)

**REQ-DPM-030:** Users SHALL be able to acknowledge alerts to dismiss them from active view.

**REQ-DPM-031:** The system SHALL maintain an alert history for 30 days.

**REQ-DPM-032:** The UI SHALL display active alerts in a dedicated notification center with filtering by severity and device.

### 2.4 Performance Reports

#### 2.4.1 Report Generation
**REQ-DPM-033:** Users SHALL be able to generate performance reports for:
- Single device over a time range
- Multiple devices over a time range
- Device group comparisons

**REQ-DPM-034:** Reports SHALL include:
- Summary statistics for each metric
- Time-series charts
- Alert history during the period
- Device uptime/availability
- Anomaly detection highlights

**REQ-DPM-035:** The system SHALL support report export in the following formats:
- PDF (with charts and tables)
- CSV (raw data)
- JSON (structured data)

**REQ-DPM-036:** Report generation SHALL be performed asynchronously for time ranges > 24 hours.

**REQ-DPM-037:** Users SHALL receive a notification when async reports are ready for download.

#### 2.4.2 Scheduled Reports
**REQ-DPM-038:** Users SHALL be able to schedule recurring reports:
- Daily, weekly, or monthly frequency
- Configurable recipients (future: email integration)
- Automatic generation and storage

**REQ-DPM-039:** Scheduled reports SHALL be stored for 90 days before automatic deletion.

### 2.5 Multi-Device Comparison

#### 2.5.1 Comparison View
**REQ-DPM-040:** The UI SHALL provide a comparison view displaying metrics for multiple devices simultaneously.

**REQ-DPM-041:** Users SHALL be able to select 2-10 devices for comparison.

**REQ-DPM-042:** The comparison view SHALL display:
- Side-by-side current metrics
- Overlaid time-series charts
- Relative performance indicators (best/worst)
- Device specifications for context

**REQ-DPM-043:** The system SHALL support filtering devices by:
- Device model
- Android version
- Status (online/offline)
- Performance tier (based on historical averages)

**REQ-DPM-044:** Users SHALL be able to save comparison configurations as presets.

---

## 3. Non-Functional Requirements

### 3.1 Performance
**REQ-DPM-NFR-001:** Metric collection SHALL NOT impact device performance by more than 2% CPU usage.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-002:** The monitoring service SHALL support 100+ concurrent device connections.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-003:** Database queries for historical data SHALL return results within 3 seconds for 30-day ranges.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-004:** The UI SHALL render charts with 10,000+ data points without lag.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-005:** WebSocket message latency SHALL be < 100ms under normal network conditions.

### 3.2 Scalability
**REQ-DPM-NFR-006:** The system SHALL scale horizontally to support 1000+ devices across multiple monitoring service instances.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-007:** Database partitioning SHALL support efficient queries across 1TB+ of historical data.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-008:** Redis cache SHALL be configured with LRU eviction and 2GB minimum memory allocation.

### 3.3 Reliability
**REQ-DPM-NFR-009:** The monitoring service SHALL have 99.9% uptime.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-010:** Metric collection failures SHALL NOT crash the monitoring service.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-011:** The system SHALL implement circuit breakers for database and Redis connections.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-012:** All critical operations SHALL be logged for debugging and audit purposes.

### 3.4 Security
**REQ-DPM-NFR-013:** All API endpoints SHALL require authentication via JWT tokens.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-014:** WebSocket connections SHALL be authenticated before streaming metrics.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-015:** Alert configurations SHALL be scoped to user permissions (admin vs. regular user).

**REQ-DPM-NFR-016:** Exported reports SHALL NOT contain sensitive device information unless user has appropriate permissions.

### 3.5 Maintainability
**REQ-DPM-NFR-017:** The monitoring service SHALL be implemented as a separate microservice in Go.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-018:** The codebase SHALL include unit tests with 80%+ coverage.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-019:** The system SHALL expose Prometheus metrics for observability.

**REQ-DPM-NFR-020:** Configuration SHALL be externalized via environment variables and config files.

---

## 4. Integration Requirements

### 4.1 Device Service Integration
**REQ-DPM-INT-001:** The monitoring service SHALL integrate with the existing device-service via gRPC or REST API.

**REQ-DPM-INT-002:** The device-service SHALL provide device connection status events to the monitoring service.

**REQ-DPM-INT-003:** The monitoring service SHALL query device-service for device metadata (model, OS version, etc.).

### 4.2 API Gateway Integration
**REQ-DPM-INT-004:** All monitoring APIs SHALL be exposed through the existing api-gateway.

**REQ-DPM-INT-005:** The api-gateway SHALL implement rate limiting for monitoring endpoints (100 requests/minute per user).

### 4.3 Auth Service Integration
**REQ-DPM-INT-006:** The monitoring service SHALL validate JWT tokens via the auth-service.

**REQ-DPM-INT-007:** Permission checks SHALL be performed for alert configuration and report generation.

### 4.4 Database Integration
**REQ-DPM-INT-008:** The monitoring service SHALL use the existing PostgreSQL instance with a dedicated schema.

**REQ-DPM-INT-009:** Database migrations SHALL be managed via a migration tool (e.g., golang-migrate).

### 4.5 Redis Integration
**REQ-DPM-INT-010:** The monitoring service SHALL use the existing Redis instance with a dedicated key prefix.

**REQ-DPM-INT-011:** Redis keys SHALL have appropriate TTL values to prevent memory bloat.

---

## 5. User Interface Requirements

### 5.1 Dashboard Integration
**REQ-DPM-UI-001:** A new "Performance" section SHALL be added to the main navigation sidebar.

**REQ-DPM-UI-002:** The dashboard SHALL display a performance overview widget showing:
- Number of devices with active alerts
- Average CPU/RAM usage across all devices
- Devices with critical status

### 5.2 Device Detail View
**REQ-DPM-UI-003:** The existing device detail view SHALL be enhanced with a "Performance" tab.

**REQ-DPM-UI-004:** The performance tab SHALL display:
- Real-time metrics with gauges
- Historical charts (last 24 hours by default)
- Recent alerts for the device
- Quick actions (export report, configure alerts)

### 5.3 Performance Monitoring Page
**REQ-DPM-UI-005:** A dedicated performance monitoring page SHALL provide:
- Multi-device comparison view
- Advanced filtering and search
- Bulk alert configuration
- Report generation interface

### 5.4 Alert Center
**REQ-DPM-UI-006:** A notification center SHALL display:
- Active alerts (unacknowledged)
- Alert history with filtering
- Alert statistics (count by severity, device)

### 5.5 Responsive Design
**REQ-DPM-UI-007:** All performance monitoring UI components SHALL be responsive and work on desktop screens (1920x1080 minimum).

**REQ-DPM-UI-008:** Charts SHALL be interactive with touch/mouse support for zoom and pan.

---

## 6. Data Requirements

### 6.1 Database Schema

#### 6.1.1 performance_metrics Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE performance_metrics (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    device_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES devices(id),
    metric_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    metric_value NUMERIC(10, 2) NOT NULL,
    metadata JSONB,
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);
```

#### 6.1.2 alert_thresholds Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE alert_thresholds (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    device_id UUID REFERENCES devices(id), -- NULL for global
    metric_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    warning_threshold NUMERIC(10, 2),
    critical_threshold NUMERIC(10, 2),
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

#### 6.1.3 alerts Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE alerts (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    device_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES devices(id),
    metric_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    severity VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, -- 'warning' or 'critical'
    metric_value NUMERIC(10, 2) NOT NULL,
    threshold_value NUMERIC(10, 2) NOT NULL,
    acknowledged BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
    acknowledged_at TIMESTAMP,
    acknowledged_by UUID REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

#### 6.1.4 performance_reports Table
```sql
CREATE TABLE performance_reports (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    report_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, -- 'single', 'multi', 'comparison'
    device_ids UUID[] NOT NULL,
    time_range_start TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    time_range_end TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    format VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, -- 'pdf', 'csv', 'json'
    file_path TEXT,
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, -- 'pending', 'completed', 'failed'
    created_by UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    completed_at TIMESTAMP
);
```

### 6.2 Redis Data Structures

**REQ-DPM-DATA-001:** Recent metrics cache:
- Key: `perf:metrics:{device_id}:{metric_type}`
- Type: Sorted Set (score = timestamp)
- TTL: 5 minutes

**REQ-DPM-DATA-002:** Alert cooldown tracking:
- Key: `perf:alert:cooldown:{device_id}:{metric_type}`
- Type: String
- TTL: 5 minutes

**REQ-DPM-DATA-003:** Active device connections:
- Key: `perf:active:{device_id}`
- Type: Hash (last_seen, connection_id)
- TTL: 30 seconds (refreshed on each metric)

---

## 7. API Requirements

### 7.1 REST API Endpoints

#### 7.1.1 Metrics API
```
GET /api/v1/performance/devices/{deviceId}/metrics/current
GET /api/v1/performance/devices/{deviceId}/metrics/history?start={timestamp}&end={timestamp}&type={metric_type}
GET /api/v1/performance/devices/metrics/summary?deviceIds={ids}&start={timestamp}&end={timestamp}
```

#### 7.1.2 Alerts API
```
GET /api/v1/performance/alerts?status={active|acknowledged}&severity={warning|critical}
POST /api/v1/performance/alerts/{alertId}/acknowledge
GET /api/v1/performance/alerts/thresholds
POST /api/v1/performance/alerts/thresholds
PUT /api/v1/performance/alerts/thresholds/{thresholdId}
DELETE /api/v1/performance/alerts/thresholds/{thresholdId}
```

#### 7.1.3 Reports API
```
POST /api/v1/performance/reports/generate
GET /api/v1/performance/reports
GET /api/v1/performance/reports/{reportId}
GET /api/v1/performance/reports/{reportId}/download
DELETE /api/v1/performance/reports/{reportId}
```

### 7.2 WebSocket API

#### 7.2.1 Metrics Stream
```
WS /ws/performance/metrics/{deviceId}

Message format:
{
  "type": "metric_update",
  "device_id": "uuid",
  "timestamp": "2026-05-16T13:30:00Z",
  "metrics": {
    "cpu_usage": 45.2,
    "ram_usage": 62.8,
    "battery_level": 85,
    "temperature": 38.5
  }
}
```

#### 7.2.2 Alert Stream
```
WS /ws/performance/alerts

Message format:
{
  "type": "alert",
  "alert_id": "uuid",
  "device_id": "uuid",
  "device_name": "Pixel 6",
  "metric_type": "cpu_usage",
  "severity": "warning",
  "current_value": 85.3,
  "threshold_value": 80.0,
  "timestamp": "2026-05-16T13:30:00Z"
}
```

---

## 8. Dependencies

### 8.1 External Dependencies
- ADB (Android Debug Bridge) - already available
- PostgreSQL 14+ with TimescaleDB extension (optional, for better time-series performance)
- Redis 6+
- Go 1.21+ for backend service
- React 18+ for UI components
- Chart.js or Recharts for data visualization

### 8.2 Internal Dependencies
- device-service (device connection management)
- auth-service (authentication/authorization)
- api-gateway (API routing)
- stream-service (WebSocket infrastructure)

---

## 9. Constraints and Assumptions

### 9.1 Constraints
- Must work with existing ADB infrastructure
- Cannot require root access on devices
- Must not interfere with existing device control functionality
- Limited to metrics available via ADB commands
- Desktop app only (no mobile/web version in this phase)

### 9.2 Assumptions
- Devices remain connected during monitoring sessions
- Network latency between device-service and devices is < 100ms
- PostgreSQL has sufficient storage for 90 days of metrics (estimate: 1KB per metric × 4 metrics × 1 reading/sec × 100 devices × 90 days ≈ 3.1TB)
- Users have sufficient permissions to view device metrics
- ADB commands return consistent output format across Android versions

---

## 10. Open Questions

1. **Q:** Should we support custom metric collection via user-defined ADB commands?
   **Status:** Pending product decision

2. **Q:** Do we need real-time alerting via email/SMS, or is in-app sufficient for MVP?
   **Status:** In-app sufficient for MVP, email/SMS in future phase

3. **Q:** Should historical data aggregation be configurable per user/organization?
   **Status:** Pending discussion

4. **Q:** Do we need role-based access control for viewing specific device metrics?
   **Status:** Yes, inherit from existing device permissions

5. **Q:** Should we implement anomaly detection using ML models?
   **Status:** Not in MVP, consider for future enhancement

---

## 11. Out of Scope

The following items are explicitly out of scope for this feature:
- Mobile app support for performance monitoring
- Integration with third-party monitoring tools (Datadog, New Relic, etc.)
- Predictive analytics and ML-based anomaly detection
- Custom metric plugins or extensions
- Performance monitoring for iOS devices
- Network traffic monitoring
- App-level performance profiling
- Automated remediation actions based on alerts

---

## 12. Acceptance Criteria

### 12.1 Feature Complete Criteria
- [ ] All functional requirements (REQ-DPM-001 through REQ-DPM-044) implemented
- [ ] All non-functional requirements met and verified through testing
- [ ] Integration with existing services completed and tested
- [ ] UI components implemented and responsive
- [ ] Database schema deployed and migrations tested
- [ ] API documentation completed
- [ ] Unit tests achieve 80%+ coverage
- [ ] Integration tests cover critical user flows
- [ ] Performance benchmarks meet specified targets

### 12.2 User Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] User can view real-time metrics for any connected device
- [ ] User can view historical metrics for the past 90 days
- [ ] User can configure alert thresholds and receive notifications
- [ ] User can generate and export performance reports
- [ ] User can compare performance across multiple devices
- [ ] System handles 100+ concurrent devices without degradation
- [ ] Alerts are delivered within 5 seconds of threshold breach
- [ ] UI updates within 2 seconds of metric collection
